title: Simpson And Delilah

jokes.text: Hitler! / North Dakota?
jokes.setup: Quiz show question about North Dakota's capital named after a German ruler
jokes.punchline: Bart immediately shouts 'Hitler!' while Homer confusedly asks 'North Dakota?'
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Bart's inappropriate confidence and Homer's geographic ignorance. Economical and character-specific.

jokes.text: I'm still beating you, boy
jokes.setup: Homer got the answer wrong and is clearly losing
jokes.punchline: Claims victory despite being wrong
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer delusion - his inability to admit defeat even when obviously wrong.

jokes.text: Breakthrough? There's been a breakthrough?!
jokes.setup: TV commercial mentions 'breakthrough in hair regrowth'
jokes.punchline: Homer's desperate, overly excited reaction to advertising language
jokes.explanation: Homer's susceptibility to marketing speak, perfectly capturing his desperation about baldness.

jokes.text: Some women find bald men quite virile. / Weren't you listening? This is a breakthrough.
jokes.setup: Marge tries to comfort Homer about baldness
jokes.punchline: Homer completely dismisses her support, obsessed with the 'breakthrough'
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer dismissing Marge's wisdom while chasing a scam - good character dynamic.

jokes.text: However, any hair growth while using it will be coincidental.
jokes.setup: Salesman offers cheaper alternative to $1000 product
jokes.punchline: Casually admits the cheap version doesn't work at all
jokes.explanation: Brilliant corporate doublespeak - the casual admission of worthlessness is perfectly delivered deadpan.

jokes.text: Stop whining, chrome dome.
jokes.setup: Homer complaining about food
jokes.punchline: Barney calls him 'chrome dome' - casual cruelty about baldness
jokes.explanation: Good insult that hits Homer's insecurity - Barney's casual cruelty is character-appropriate.

jokes.text: If I had hair, you wouldn't say that.
jokes.setup: Barney insults Homer's baldness
jokes.punchline: Homer's pathetic response connecting everything to his hair
jokes.explanation: Homer's delusional belief that hair would change everything - sad and funny character moment.

jokes.text: So he has one less ivory back scratcher.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Burns losing $1000
jokes.punchline: Casual reference to Burns' ridiculous luxury purchases
jokes.explanation: Great specific detail about Burns' wealth - ivory back scratcher is absurd and memorable.

jokes.text: Meet me in the alley in 15 minutes. Come alone.
jokes.setup: Homer asking about insurance fraud at counter
jokes.punchline: Clerk responds like arranging a criminal deal
jokes.explanation: Brilliant subversion - treating routine insurance fraud like a spy thriller. Great economy and surprise.

jokes.text: Get a haircut, you hippie!
jokes.setup: Homer greets neighbors with his new hair
jokes.punchline: Ned immediately tells him to cut it
jokes.explanation: Perfect irony - Homer finally gets hair and is immediately told to cut it. Great Flanders characterization.

jokes.text: Good morning, Moe's Tavern! It's the president.
jokes.setup: Homer calling Moe's with newfound confidence
jokes.punchline: Claims to be the president
jokes.explanation: Homer's confidence boost leads to ridiculous claims - shows how hair changed his self-image.

jokes.text: Just between us girls, he hasn't been this frisky in years.
jokes.setup: Marge talking to her sisters about Homer
jokes.punchline: TMI about their love life improvement
jokes.explanation: Marge's oversharing with her sisters - uncomfortable but character-appropriate intimacy.

jokes.text: Patty, stop drooling. / Look who's talking.
jokes.setup: Both sisters are attracted to new Homer
jokes.punchline: They accuse each other of drooling over him
jokes.explanation: Great reversal - the sisters who usually hate Homer are now attracted to him. Good sibling dynamic.

jokes.text: You look different, Homer. Lose weight? You got a tan. I know what it is, a new tie.
jokes.setup: Sisters notice Homer looks different
jokes.punchline: They guess everything except the obvious - his hair
jokes.explanation: Good escalating guesses that miss the obvious answer - builds anticipation for reveal.

jokes.text: Looks like Homer Simpson, only more dynamic and resourceful.
jokes.setup: Burns sees Homer with hair
jokes.punchline: Describes Homer as 'dynamic and resourceful' - the opposite of reality
jokes.explanation: Great irony - hair completely changes Burns' perception despite Homer being identical otherwise.

jokes.text: Simpson? An unspoiled lump of clay to mold in my own image.
jokes.setup: Burns considers promoting Homer
jokes.punchline: Describes him as raw material to shape
jokes.explanation: Burns' megalomaniacal language is always good - 'unspoiled lump of clay' is perfectly pretentious.

jokes.text: Backrubs for harried executives. I'll show you.
jokes.setup: Karl interviewing for assistant position, asked about specialties
jokes.punchline: Immediately offers to demonstrate backrubs
jokes.explanation: Karl's immediate physical intimacy offer is both professional and inappropriate - good character intro.

jokes.text: Hire him. / I'll call you back.
jokes.setup: Homer on phone with Marge when Karl introduces himself
jokes.punchline: Immediately hires Karl mid-conversation
jokes.explanation: Homer's instant decision-making with hair-induced confidence - good economy and character moment.

jokes.text: You don't belong here. You don't belong here.
jokes.setup: Karl psychoanalyzing Homer's insecurity
jokes.punchline: Repeating Homer's inner voice of self-doubt
jokes.explanation: Karl immediately identifies Homer's core insecurity - good character insight and building to therapy moment.

jokes.text: You're a fraud and a phony, and they'll find you out.
jokes.setup: Karl asks about Homer's self-doubt
jokes.punchline: Homer's brutally honest self-assessment
jokes.explanation: Homer's surprising self-awareness about his incompetence - rare moment of clarity from him.

jokes.text: With the way you talk into your chest... the way you smother yourself in lime-green polyester.
jokes.setup: Karl diagnosing Homer's insecurity signals
jokes.punchline: Specific observations about Homer's body language and fashion
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly specific observations - 'lime-green polyester' and 'talk into your chest' are perfect details.

jokes.text: I am nature's greatest miracle!
jokes.setup: Karl coaching Homer's self-affirmation
jokes.punchline: Homer shouting the ridiculous affirmation
jokes.explanation: The absurdity of Homer declaring himself 'nature's greatest miracle' is both empowering and ridiculous.

jokes.text: A man's suit should make him a prince. It should cry out: 'Here I am. Don't judge me, love me.'
jokes.setup: Karl taking Homer suit shopping
jokes.punchline: Overly dramatic philosophy about clothing
jokes.explanation: Karl's pretentious fashion philosophy is perfectly over-the-top - 'Don't judge me, love me' is memorable.

jokes.text: You, conceal it.
jokes.setup: Karl telling Homer to stand naturally, let it all hang out
jokes.punchline: Immediately tells another customer to conceal themselves
jokes.explanation: Perfect comedic timing and economy - the immediate contradiction is brutally honest and funny.

jokes.text: I love you, Karl-- Marge.
jokes.setup: Homer overwhelmed by Karl's help
jokes.punchline: Almost says he loves Karl instead of Marge
jokes.explanation: Great misdirection joke - Homer's confusion about who deserves his gratitude/love.

jokes.text: Well, sir, for one thing... we had a problem with fish sticks at lunch.
jokes.setup: Burns asks Homer how to improve workplace productivity
jokes.punchline: Homer brings up his earlier cafeteria complaint
jokes.explanation: Good callback to earlier whining, showing Homer's limited perspective on workplace issues.

jokes.text: You only get this tiny cup of sauce, and I always run out.
jokes.setup: Homer explaining the fish stick problem
jokes.punchline: His detailed concern about sauce portions
jokes.explanation: Homer's focus on trivial personal complaints when asked about major business issues.

jokes.text: Can't you see? A happy worker is a busy worker. Three cents' worth of sauce could save us hours in labor.
jokes.setup: Homer's trivial sauce complaint
jokes.punchline: Burns treats it as brilliant business insight
jokes.explanation: Brilliant reversal - Burns finding profound wisdom in Homer's petty complaint. Great business speak parody.

jokes.text: I like the cut of your jib.
jokes.setup: Burns impressed with Homer's 'insight'
jokes.punchline: Uses old-fashioned nautical expression
jokes.explanation: Classic Burns antiquated language - 'cut of your jib' is perfectly in character for his old-fashioned speech.

jokes.text: Accidents decreased by the number Simpson is known to have caused.
jokes.setup: Smithers reporting on workplace improvements
jokes.punchline: Attributes safety improvement to Homer's absence from dangerous work
jokes.explanation: Perfect Smithers deadpan delivery - the implication that Homer causes all the accidents is brilliantly understated.

jokes.text: I was watching the DuMont last night and I caught a documentary on Rommel.
jokes.setup: Burns making small talk in the executive washroom
jokes.punchline: References obscure old TV network and Nazi general
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns characterization - his age shown through obsolete TV reference and casual Nazi documentary viewing.

jokes.text: $1000. Dimoxinil. To keep brain from freezing.
jokes.setup: Smithers reading Homer's insurance claim
jokes.punchline: The absurd medical justification for hair product
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd medical justification - 'to keep brain from freezing' is perfect Homer logic.

jokes.text: An absence of mood swings and stability in my life. / How about a pony? / Okay!
jokes.setup: Lisa gives mature answer about what she wants
jokes.punchline: Homer offers pony, Lisa immediately abandons principles
jokes.explanation: Great subversion - Lisa's mature request immediately abandoned for childhood desire. Good character contradiction.

jokes.text: And I was going to buy that ivory backscratcher.
jokes.setup: Burns upset about losing $1000
jokes.punchline: References the ivory backscratcher from earlier
jokes.explanation: Good callback to earlier joke about Burns' ridiculous luxury purchases.

jokes.text: I did what any soldier would do when a grenade threatens his commander. I threw myself upon it and bore its terrible brunt.
jokes.setup: Karl explaining why he took blame for Homer
jokes.punchline: Overly dramatic military metaphor for office situation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Karl dialogue - overblown heroic language for mundane office politics. 'Terrible brunt' is especially good.

jokes.text: Hey, what's happening, hepcats? / No way. It's gotta be a fake. It's like reals-ville, daddy-o.
jokes.setup: Bart shows up at school with fake hair
jokes.punchline: Uses outdated slang to seem cool
jokes.explanation: Good generational humor - Bart using 1950s slang thinking it's cool. 'Reals-ville' is particularly good.

jokes.text: You've ruined me, you've crippled your family and baldness is hereditary!
jokes.setup: Homer angry at Bart for ruining his toupee
jokes.punchline: Escalating threats ending with genetic doom
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation - from personal to family to genetic curse. 'Baldness is hereditary!' is both funny and cruel.

jokes.text: The big words are spelled phonetically.
jokes.setup: Karl prepared Homer's speech on cards
jokes.punchline: Admits the big words need phonetic spelling
jokes.explanation: Good acknowledgment of Homer's limitations - Karl knows his audience and prepares accordingly.

jokes.text: It was never the hair. You did it, because you believed you could.
jokes.setup: Homer thinks he can't succeed without hair
jokes.punchline: Karl reveals the real source of Homer's temporary success
jokes.explanation: Good thematic moment - reveals that confidence, not hair, was the real change. Classic self-help wisdom.

jokes.text: How do you know? / Mother taught me never to kiss a fool.
jokes.setup: Homer asks how Karl knows he's not a fool
jokes.punchline: Karl's intimate and flattering response
jokes.explanation: Clever way to reveal Karl kissed Homer while delivering a compliment. Good structure and character moment.

jokes.text: Yet this company does this every day.
jokes.setup: Homer demonstrates something crazy-looking to the board
jokes.punchline: Points out the company's hypocritical practices
jokes.explanation: Decent corporate satire moment but not particularly memorable or clever.

jokes.text: This bald man has no ideas. / If it's a joke, I'm not laughing.
jokes.setup: Homer giving same speech but now bald
jokes.punchline: Audience completely rejects identical content
jokes.explanation: Shows the cruel reality of appearance-based judgment - same speech, different reception.

jokes.text: How old do you think I am? / I don't know. 102?
jokes.setup: Burns asks Homer to guess his age
jokes.punchline: Homer's brutally honest overestimate
jokes.explanation: Homer's tactless honesty is perfectly in character - no attempt to flatter Burns.

jokes.text: ...my glory was a bright shock of strawberry blond curls.
jokes.setup: Burns describing his youthful hair
jokes.punchline: Vivid, incongruous description of Burns with beautiful hair
jokes.explanation: Great specific visual detail that's impossible to imagine on Burns. The phrasing 'bright shock' is well-chosen.

tmdb_overview: Homer uses his medical insurance to buy Dimoxinil, a miracle hair-growth formula. After growing luxurious locks, he's given a promotion which allows him to hire a secretary named Karl and makes grand promises to his family at home.

guest_stars.name: Pamela Hayden
guest_stars.character: Milhouse Van Houten / Applicant / Woman in Audience (voice)

insight: Homer's hair-loss vanity plot sustains 81.9 laughs across 44 jokes with consistent character comedy.
